proposals.title: Private Multi-Group Learning via Stochastic Optimization with Adaptive Gradient Calibration
proposals.motivation: The deployment of complex machine learning models in sensitive domains requires both strong differential privacy guarantees and fairness across diverse subpopulations. Pre-2026, private stochastic optimization methods often treat privacy and fairness as separate constraints, which can lead to degraded utility or insufficient protection for minority groups when training on high-dimensional, non-convex objectives.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating multicalibration objectives directly into the private stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD) update rule, we can train complex models that simultaneously satisfy rigorous differential privacy and provide strong multi-group fairness guarantees without a significant loss in overall utility.
proposals.method: We will develop a novel private optimization algorithm, DP-Calibrated SGD, that modifies the DP-SGD framework. At each iteration, we will compute a composite gradient: a standard clipped and noised gradient for the primary loss, plus a carefully calibrated correction term derived from the multicalibration error of the current model across pre-defined groups. The calibration term will be computed using a private, batched version of the HappyMap procedure, ensuring the entire process remains within a tight (ε, δ)-differential privacy budget. We will analyze convergence for convex and non-convex losses and test on large-scale vision and language datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified algorithmic framework for private and fair learning, bridging the differential privacy and algorithmic fairness literatures. It will offer a rigorous theoretical analysis of the privacy-utility-fairness trade-off and deliver an open-source, scalable implementation for training deep neural networks with certified multi-group guarantees.
proposals.novelty: While DP-SGD and multicalibration are established, their integration into a single, end-to-end private stochastic optimization procedure is novel. The key innovation is the adaptive, in-training calibration that proactively shapes the gradient updates to satisfy multi-group constraints under noise addition, moving beyond post-processing or simplistic fairness penalties.

proposals.title: Information-Theoretic Calibration: A Unified Framework for Multi-Group Fairness and Privacy
proposals.motivation: Differential privacy provides strong worst-case guarantees but can be overly conservative, while multi-group fairness and multicalibration frameworks ensure accurate predictions across many subpopulations without a formal privacy analysis. This creates a tension: achieving fine-grained statistical fairness may inherently leak information about individuals in small groups. A rigorous, information-theoretic analysis is needed to quantify this leakage and design algorithms that navigate the fairness-privacy trade-off.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mutual information and rate-distortion theory can provide a unified, composable language to measure the privacy cost of multi-group fairness constraints, and that this framework will enable the design of algorithms that explicitly bound both group-level inaccuracy and individual-level information leakage.
proposals.method: We will formalize the statistical query released by a multicalibrated predictor as a communication channel. Using mutual information I(S; R) between the sensitive dataset S and the released model R, we will derive leakage bounds. We will then apply rate-distortion theory, treating the fairness constraints as a distortion measure, to characterize the minimal achievable leakage for a given fairness guarantee. This will lead to new algorithmic designs that integrate noise addition, inspired by differential privacy, directly into the calibration loop to satisfy an information-theoretic privacy budget.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal, composable privacy analysis for multi-group fairness methods. It will yield new, practical algorithms that offer tunable, information-theoretic privacy guarantees alongside multicalibration. The framework will also clarify the inherent leakage of subgroup fairness, answering foundational questions raised in recent debates on multiplicity and privacy.
proposals.novelty: While information theory has been applied to differential privacy, its use to analyze the privacy of multi-group fairness constraints—which are distributional, not purely worst-case—is novel. Bridging the specific algorithmic paradigms of multicalibration/HappyMap with rate-distortion theory represents a significant theoretical advance.

proposals.title: Private Inference on Tiny Datasets: A Multiplicative Weights Framework for Composition and Calibration
proposals.motivation: Differential privacy (DP) is the gold standard for privacy-preserving data analysis, but its standard additive noise mechanisms can obliterate statistical utility on very small datasets (e.g., n < 100), common in sensitive fields like medicine or sociology. Pre-2026 work has largely focused on large-scale data, leaving a critical gap in the trustworthy analysis of micro-data where the signal-to-noise ratio is catastrophically low.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the composition of queries and incorporating a multicalibration-style weighting scheme, we can design DP algorithms for tiny datasets that provide meaningful, finite-sample confidence intervals and reduce the effective variance of noise compared to naive baseline mechanisms.
proposals.method: We will adapt the multiplicative weights framework, central to multicalibration and the 'From Pseudorandomness to Multi-Group Fairness' paper, to the DP setting. The core algorithm will maintain a distribution over data points (or synthetic representatives) updated via a loss function that penalizes both privacy violation (via a DP composition accountant) and utility error. For a small, static dataset, we will iteratively answer an adaptively chosen sequence of queries, using the weights to 'amplify' the signal from consistent patterns while adding noise calibrated to the evolving privacy budget and the current weighted dataset size.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled framework for DP analysis tailored to tiny sample sizes, yielding concrete algorithms with provable utility-privacy trade-offs. It will extend the conceptual link between fairness multicalibration and privacy, demonstrating how weighting individuals can manage composition and improve effective sample size in micro-data.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the multiplicative weights update—a tool for fairness—directly to the problem of DP noise reduction for fixed, small `n`, and in formally analyzing the composition of queries in this severely constrained regime, moving beyond the large-n asymptotic guarantees that dominate the pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Auditing Differential Privacy: Statistical Methods for Empirical ε Estimation in Deployed Systems
proposals.motivation: Differential privacy (DP) provides rigorous mathematical guarantees, but in practice, systems claiming DP are often 'black boxes' with a stated privacy parameter ε. There is a critical need for post-hoc, empirical methods to audit these claims, ensuring accountability and trust. Current pre-2026 work focuses on designing private algorithms, not on independently verifying their deployed privacy loss.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by framing privacy auditing as a statistical estimation problem and leveraging techniques from hypothesis testing and multicalibration, we can develop rigorous, data-driven methods to produce confidence intervals for the empirical ε of a deployed system.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the 'attack' or distinguishing adversary framework from privacy literature into a formal estimation procedure. The core method involves designing a suite of tailored statistical tests that attempt to distinguish between outputs on neighboring datasets. We will employ techniques from multicalibration (HappyMap) to ensure our audit is robust across diverse subpopulations (multi-group fairness), preventing an average-case ε from hiding high privacy loss for specific individuals or groups. The final output will be a calibrated confidence interval for the effective ε.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first general methodological framework for empirical DP auditing, moving the field from trust-by-design to verifiable claims. It will yield practical tools for regulators, auditors, and data subjects to assess real-world privacy guarantees, strengthening algorithmic accountability.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines specific attacks, our approach systematically inverts the DP definition to create a *measurement* tool. The key novelty is the integration of multicalibration principles to ensure the audit's fairness and rigor across populations, addressing the 'individual' and 'multi' aspects of privacy risk highlighted in Dwork's recent themes.

proposals.title: Robust Synthetic Data Generation Under Noisy Statistical Queries: A Differential Privacy and Multicalibration Approach
proposals.motivation: The 2020 US Census deployed differential privacy (DP) for its official statistics, introducing a known, controlled form of measurement error. Many downstream applications, like policy modeling and research, rely on generating synthetic microdata that is consistent with these noisy published statistics. Existing generation methods often treat published statistics as ground truth, which can propagate and amplify errors, leading to biased or unreliable synthetic datasets.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by explicitly modeling the error distribution of DP-protected statistics (or other known error processes) within the data generation process, we can produce synthetic populations that are more robust—maintaining fidelity to the underlying population distribution while satisfying multi-group fairness and privacy constraints.
proposals.method: We will extend the multicalibration and HappyMap framework to condition not on exact statistics, but on confidence intervals derived from the DP noise distribution. The core algorithm will iteratively adjust a generative model (e.g., a probabilistic graphical model or a GAN) to ensure its outputs are calibrated not just for demographic subgroups, but across plausible realizations of the noisy measurements. We will integrate this with the post-processing immunity of DP to ensure the final synthetic data does not compromise the privacy guarantee of the source statistics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled method for creating high-utility synthetic data from error-prone official statistics, directly addressing a practical challenge of the modern DP census. It will broaden the theory of multicalibration to stochastic constraints and deliver an open-source tool for robust synthetic data generation.
proposals.novelty: While multicalibration ensures fairness across subgroups for exact statistics, applying it to noisy, DP-infused statistics with the goal of robust generation is novel. This bridges the 'multi' themes of multi-group fairness and multiplicity in data releases with the core constraint of differential privacy in a new, practically vital way.

proposals.title: Tightening Bounds for Multi-Group Fairness and Multicalibration via Primal-Dual and Submodular Optimization
proposals.motivation: Recent frameworks for multi-group fairness and multicalibration, such as those in Dwork et al.'s work, rely on combinatorial optimization algorithms to find and correct violations across exponentially many subgroups. While these algorithms are foundational, their theoretical performance guarantees—particularly runtime and approximation ratios relative to the optimal fair predictor—are not yet tight. Improving these bounds is crucial for understanding the inherent computational-accuracy trade-offs in providing strong, granular fairness and privacy guarantees, which informs both algorithmic design and policy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primal-dual structure underlying multi-group fair learning and the error decomposition in multicalibration can be leveraged to reframe the core optimization problems, enabling the application of advanced techniques from submodular optimization and online learning to achieve near-optimal runtime and approximation guarantees.
proposals.method: We will analyze the combinatorial search over groups in multicalibration and multi-group fairness as a constrained submodular cover or a zero-sum game. First, we will employ the multiplicative weights update framework to derive faster convergence rates for the meta-algorithm. Second, we will explore the submodularity of the violation function to design greedy algorithms with improved approximation factors. Finally, we will integrate these with differential privacy mechanisms to provide end-to-end theoretical guarantees on runtime, accuracy, and privacy for the combined system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first near-tight theoretical bounds on the computational complexity and approximation quality for achieving multi-group fairness and multicalibration. It will establish a clearer landscape of the inherent trade-offs in these frameworks, guiding practitioners on the limits of efficient fair learning. The results will also strengthen the theoretical foundations connecting pseudorandomness, fairness, and privacy.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly connecting the structure of multicalibration objectives to submodular optimization and game-theoretic dynamics to tighten bounds, moving beyond the generic regret minimization analysis used in initial constructions. This approach is non-obvious as it requires a fresh combinatorial perspective on the group violation structure.

proposals.title: Homeostatic Fairness: Dynamic Multi-Group Parity via Feedback Control
proposals.motivation: Current algorithmic fairness frameworks often impose static constraints, which can become brittle or inappropriate under real-world distribution shifts or evolving societal values. This mismatch can lead to performance degradation or the perpetuation of inequity in dynamic environments, such as lending or hiring systems. A principled, adaptive approach is needed to maintain equitable outcomes over time without requiring constant manual re-auditing.
proposals.hypothesis: Fairness constraints modeled as homeostatic control loops, which dynamically adjust model parameters or objectives in response to measured disparities, can maintain robust multi-group fairness guarantees in non-stationary environments more effectively than static constraints.
proposals.method: We will formalize fairness metrics (e.g., calibration, equalized odds) as system states to be regulated. A two-tier controller will be designed: a fast inner loop using proportional-integral control on a multi-objective loss (e.g., dynamically weighting fairness regularization via a variant of Multi-Group Fairness or HappyMap) to correct short-term deviations. A slow outer loop will monitor for persistent distributional shifts and, using statistical tests, adapt the target 'fairness set point' itself, informed by concepts of individual fairness and differential privacy to ensure stability and prevent overfitting to transient noise.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel control-theoretic framework for dynamic fairness, yielding algorithms that provably maintain fairness under drift. It will extend the theory of multi-calibration to time-varying settings and offer a new paradigm for designing resilient, context-aware ML systems that bridge the gap between static fairness definitions and dynamic real-world deployment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored online fairness or constrained optimization, the explicit use of homeostasis and formal feedback control theory to dynamically govern the *strength* and *target* of fairness constraints represents a significant conceptual and technical departure from static fairness interventions.

proposals.title: Niche Representations: Fairness via Invariance in Regression and Unsupervised Learning
proposals.motivation: Fair representation learning has focused predominantly on classification, leaving critical gaps in regression (e.g., predicting income, risk scores) and unsupervised learning (e.g., clustering for customer segmentation). These settings are vulnerable to encoding and amplifying societal biases present in training data, leading to discriminatory outcomes. A principled framework for learning representations that are informative for the task yet invariant to protected attributes is urgently needed.
proposals.hypothesis: The principle of ecological niche modeling—learning a core model robust to biased sampling—provides a formal and effective analogy for constructing fair representations. We hypothesize that by enforcing statistical independence between learned latent features and protected attributes, we can achieve fairness in regression and uncover intrinsic data structures in unsupervised learning that are not artifacts of bias.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the multicalibration and outcome indistinguishability framework, as in Dwork's recent work, to the regression setting. For a continuous target Y, we will learn a representation Z that maximizes predictive power for Y subject to constraints ensuring Z is statistically independent of protected attributes A, measured via rigorous mutual information or correlation metrics. For unsupervised learning, we will formulate an objective for clustering or dimensionality reduction that minimizes the dependence between the discovered assignments/features and A, using adversarial training or kernel-based independence tests, ensuring the discovered structures are invariant to A.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first unified fairness framework, grounded in multicalibration and independence, for regression and unsupervised tasks. It will deliver concrete algorithms with provable guarantees on representation utility and fairness, expanding the toolkit for responsible machine learning beyond classification. The ecological analogy will offer an intuitive, interdisciplinary justification for the invariance principle.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty lies in formally bridging ecological robustness concepts to algorithmic fairness for non-classification tasks, a direct but unexplored analogy. Technically, it moves beyond group fairness definitions to enforce representation-level independence, adapting and extending Dwork's multicalibration methods to these new problem domains.

proposals.title: Pareto Frontiers of Fairness: A Multi-Objective Framework for Navigating Trade-offs Between Competing Fairness Definitions
proposals.motivation: The field of algorithmic fairness has produced numerous statistical definitions (e.g., demographic parity, equalized odds, calibration) that are often mutually incompatible. Practitioners face a difficult choice, as selecting a single constraint can obscure trade-offs and may not align with the specific ethical or legal context of a deployment. A principled framework is needed to explicitly map these trade-offs, moving beyond ad-hoc aggregation or the selection of a single fairness metric.
proposals.hypothesis: Treating distinct fairness criteria as separate objectives in a multi-objective optimization problem, alongside predictive utility, will yield a Pareto front of classifier families that clearly visualizes the inherent trade-offs, enabling context-aware model selection without collapsing fairness into a single measure.
proposals.method: We will formalize the problem as a multi-objective optimization over model parameters, with objectives for predictive loss (e.g., cross-entropy) and violations of k distinct fairness definitions (e.g., demographic parity difference, equalized odds gap). Drawing from ecology and multi-objective evolutionary algorithms (MOEAs), we will adapt algorithms like NSGA-II to approximate the Pareto front in the (k+1)-dimensional objective space. Building on Dwork et al.'s multicalibration and multi-group fairness frameworks, we will investigate connections between points on the front and the existence of low-loss, multi-accurate predictors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete computational tool for auditing and navigating fairness-utility trade-offs, offering a more nuanced alternative to constrained optimization with a single fairness metric. It will contribute a theoretical and empirical analysis of the geometry of the fairness Pareto front, linking multi-objective optimization to the multi-group fairness literature.
proposals.novelty: While multi-objective optimization is known, its application to formally navigate between *incompatible* statistical fairness definitions, inspired by ecosystem management trade-offs, is novel. This approach departs from prior work that seeks a unified compromise (e.g., through weighting) and instead makes the conflict explicit and navigable.

proposals.title: Adversary Escalation: A Multi-Group Fairness Framework via Capacity-Adaptive Ensembles
proposals.motivation: Adversarial debiasing is a leading approach for learning fair representations, but its efficacy is limited by the capacity of the adversary. A weak adversary can be easily circumvented, leading to superficial decorrelation and residual bias. This failure mode is particularly critical for multi-group fairness, where complex, intersecting protected attributes must be obscured.
proposals.hypothesis: A dynamic, capacity-adaptive ensemble of adversaries, guided by a meta-learner that escalates the adversarial challenge, will enforce a stricter, more robust independence condition between the learned representation and protected attributes than a single static adversary.
proposals.method: We propose a training framework with a primary model, an ensemble of adversarial classifiers (e.g., varying architectures like MLPs, CNNs, or simple linear probes), and a meta-learner. The meta-learner, inspired by multi-calibration's error-aware weighting, will assign higher weights to gradients from adversaries most successful at predicting protected attributes. It will also trigger the introduction of new, higher-capacity adversary architectures when ensemble performance plateaus, creating an escalating challenge. The optimization will use a minimax objective, but with the adversary's loss computed as a dynamically weighted combination from the ensemble.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, adaptive method for adversarial debiasing that provably reduces extractable protected information more effectively than prior static approaches. It will bridge concepts from multi-group fairness, multicalibration, and adversarial robustness, offering a new tool for achieving stronger fairness guarantees in complex, real-world settings.
proposals.novelty: While ensemble methods exist, the novel integration of a meta-learner for gradient re-weighting and the explicit, capacity-adaptive escalation of the adversary ensemble during training is a new direction. This moves beyond using a fixed set of adversaries to a closed-loop system that actively seeks and closes representation loopholes.

proposals.title: Inducing Invariance via Counterfactual Adversarial Augmentation: A Mechanism for Multi-Group Fairness
proposals.motivation: Adversarial debiasing aims to learn representations invariant to protected attributes, a core goal in algorithmic fairness. However, gradient-based adversarial training can be unstable and may not fully remove sensitive information, leaving residual bias. This problem is acute in multi-group settings where fairness must hold across many intersecting or fine-grained subgroups, as highlighted in recent multicalibration and multi-accuracy literature.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematically training an adversarial predictor on a counterfactually augmented dataset—where protected attributes are altered but labels are preserved—will more effectively deny the adversary a coherent signal, thereby forcing the primary model's representations to become invariant to the protected attribute by construction.
proposals.method: We will formalize a framework for generating counterfactual examples by applying minimal, label-preserving interventions on protected attributes (e.g., via causal models or realistic data transformations). The adversarial network will be trained exclusively on this augmented set, which contains contradictory signals about the correlation between the representation and the attribute. The primary model will be updated to maximize prediction accuracy while minimizing the adversary's performance on this augmented set, using techniques inspired by multicalibration to ensure robustness across subgroups. We will analyze convergence and the information-theoretic bounds on the residual signal available to the adversary.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, more stable method for achieving multi-group fairness through adversarial training, grounded in a causal data augmentation perspective. It will offer theoretical guarantees on the level of invariance achieved and connect the mechanics of adversarial debiasing to concepts of multiplicity and robustness from the multicalibration literature.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the explicit use of counterfactual data augmentation as the training environment for the adversary, an approach inspired by immunological tolerance, rather than relying solely on gradient reversal or penalties. This provides a direct mechanism to break spurious correlations and may offer stronger invariance guarantees, particularly for complex, multi-dimensional protected attributes.

proposals.title: Adversarial Feature Selection for Multicalibrated and Private Ranking
proposals.motivation: Learning-to-rank systems, critical in hiring and admissions, often perpetuate societal biases by relying on proxy features correlated with protected attributes. Post-hoc fairness interventions can be insufficient or leak sensitive information. A pre-2026 gap exists in developing ranking models that are both accurate and prevent the encoding of bias at the feature selection stage, aligning with the principle of data minimization and precluding reconstruction attacks.
proposals.hypothesis: An adversarial minimax game between a feature selector and an attribute reconstruction adversary can learn a subset of features that yields high ranking utility while formally limiting an adversary's ability to infer protected group membership, thereby producing a ranking function that satisfies multicalibration-style fairness guarantees with inherent privacy.
proposals.method: We propose a three-player game: a feature selector (a stochastic policy network), a ranker (e.g., a LambdaMART variant), and an adversary (a neural network). The selector chooses a feature subset; the ranker is trained on it to minimize a ranking loss; the adversary is trained on the *same selected features* to reconstruct protected attributes. The selector's objective is to maximize ranker accuracy minus a differential privacy-inspired penalty on adversary success, formalized via a max-min optimization. We will adapt the multicalibration framework from 'HappyMap' to provide rigorous fairness guarantees for the final ranked lists.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel adversarial feature selection framework for ranking that enforces fairness-by-design, 2) a formal connection between in-processing debiasing, multicalibration, and reconstruction privacy, and 3) a practical algorithm for deploying ranking systems that comply with pre-2026 notions of algorithmic fairness and data protection.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial debiasing exists for classification, its integration into *feature selection* for ranking is novel. The approach uniquely fuses the reconstruction attack perspective of differential privacy ('Multiplicity does not protect privacy') with the multi-group fairness guarantees of multicalibration, moving mitigation upstream in the ML pipeline.

proposals.title: Adversarial Multicalibration: A Minimax Framework for Multi-Group Fairness and Privacy
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work on algorithmic fairness, such as multicalibration, provides strong multi-group fairness guarantees but often relies on post-processing or constrained optimization that may limit model utility or flexibility. Concurrently, adversarial debiasing has shown promise in reducing reliance on protected attributes but lacks the rigorous, quantifiable fairness guarantees of the multicalibration framework. Bridging this gap is crucial for developing powerful, trainable models that provably satisfy fairness criteria across many subgroups.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a minimax training objective, where a predictor is trained to be simultaneously accurate for a task and maximally uninformative to an adversary trying to infer protected attributes, can be formally linked to and can achieve multicalibration guarantees. Furthermore, we hypothesize that this adversarial formulation can provide a novel lens for analyzing privacy leakage in fair models, connecting to differential privacy.
proposals.method: We will design a neural network architecture with a primary predictor and an adversarial network that takes the predictor's representations or outputs as input. The training will follow a minimax game, minimizing prediction loss while maximizing the adversary's loss for protected attribute prediction. We will integrate this with the 'HappyMap' multicalibration framework, theoretically analyzing the equilibrium conditions of the game to derive multicalibration bounds. The adversary's architecture and access will be varied to explore trade-offs between utility, fairness, and potential information leakage about individuals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, trainable method for achieving multicalibration with deep networks, expanding the toolkit beyond boosting-based approaches. It will formally connect adversarial debiasing to rigorous multi-group fairness definitions, strengthening the theoretical foundations of the former. Finally, it will initiate an analysis of the inherent privacy-utility-fairness trade-offs within this adversarial framework, extending the 'multiplicity does not protect privacy' discussion.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial fairness is known, its direct formulation as a method to achieve and analyze multicalibration is novel. The explicit aim to use the adversarial framework as a bridge between the trainability of deep networks, the rigorous guarantees of multicalibration, and the privacy concerns highlighted in Dwork's recent work represents a significant synthesis of her core themes.

proposals.title: Fair Free Energy: A Thermodynamic Framework for Fair Representation Learning with Variational Autoencoders
proposals.motivation: Learning fair data representations is critical for mitigating algorithmic bias, but existing methods often lack a principled framework for trading off representation quality, fairness, and model capacity. Drawing an analogy to statistical physics, where systems naturally minimize free energy to balance order and disorder, offers a rigorous foundation for this trade-off. This approach directly connects to Cynthia Dwork's recent work on multicalibration and multi-group fairness, which seeks robust, composable fairness guarantees.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating the VAE objective as the minimization of a 'fair free energy' functional, where a fairness penalty acts as internal energy and the KL divergence acts as negative entropy, will provide a tunable, theoretically grounded mechanism to learn representations that optimally balance data fidelity with fairness constraints.
proposals.method: We will define the fair free energy as F = L_recon + λ * I(z; a) - T * D_KL(q(z|x) || p(z)), where I(z;a) is the mutual information between latent codes z and protected attribute a, estimated via variational bounds, λ controls fairness strength, and 'temperature' T controls the entropy term's influence. We will adapt the VAE training algorithm to minimize F, exploring fairness penalties based on demographic parity or equalized odds. The core technical challenge is efficiently estimating and controlling I(z;a) during stochastic gradient descent.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel thermodynamic framework for fair representation learning, providing a clear interpretation of hyperparameters (λ as an energy weight, T as a temperature). It will yield a new family of fair VAE algorithms with a principled trade-off mechanism. The analysis will connect representation fairness to concepts of statistical equilibrium, offering new theoretical insights.
proposals.novelty: While VAEs for fair representation learning exist, the explicit reformulation of the loss as a thermodynamic free energy minimization, with a tunable fairness 'energy' and capacity 'entropy', is novel. This provides a non-obvious, physics-inspired lens to unify and interpret fairness-utility trade-offs.

proposals.title: Causal Multi-Calibration: Enforcing Fairness via Structural Causal Models
proposals.motivation: Current fairness definitions, including multi-group fairness and multicalibration, are largely associational, potentially failing to account for the causal mechanisms that generate biased data. This can lead to interventions that are statistically fair but perpetuate or introduce new injustices by manipulating effects rather than root causes. A causal framework is necessary to define fairness with respect to legitimate and illegitimate pathways of influence, moving beyond correlations to target the structural sources of disparity.
proposals.hypothesis: That fairness constraints defined on causal graphs—specifically by conditioning on or intervening on non-discriminatory variables—can be operationalized within the multicalibration framework, leading to algorithms that satisfy both causal fairness criteria and strong statistical guarantees across overlapping subpopulations.
proposals.method: We will extend the HappyMap framework to incorporate structural causal models (SCMs). The method will define a causal notion of multi-accuracy, requiring calibrated predictions conditional on causal parents representing permissible factors. We will develop a causal multi-calibration algorithm that iteratively audits and corrects predictions not just on groups defined by observable features, but on counterfactual distributions simulated via the SCM, using techniques from do-calculus and potential outcomes. The algorithm will maintain the oracle-efficient, boosting-based structure of HappyMap but with auditors constrained by the causal graph.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal bridge between causal fairness definitions and the rigorous, multi-group guarantee framework of multicalibration. It will yield a practical algorithm for causal fairness that offers strong, quantifiable error bounds across many intersecting groups, addressing a key limitation of prior causal methods which often focus on a single protected attribute. The results will clarify when associational fairness suffices and when causal reasoning is essential.
proposals.novelty: While causal fairness and multicalibration are both active pre-2026 research areas, their synthesis is novel. The key innovation is enforcing causal constraints via a scalable, multigroup auditing procedure, moving beyond single-attribute or total population causal definitions to a richly granular guarantee consistent with causal pathways.

proposals.title: FairRank-Select: A Multicalibration Framework for Interpretable and Fair Feature Selection in Visual Ranking
proposals.motivation: Automated ranking from visual data, such as assessing professional expertise from portfolios or surgical skill from recordings, is increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains. Pre-2026, fairness interventions in deep learning often operate on final representations or outputs, lacking mechanisms to identify and isolate which specific visual features drive both predictive performance and bias. This creates uninterpretable systems where fairness is a post-hoc constraint, not a property of the selected features used for ranking.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a multicalibration-based fairness objective directly into a learning-to-rank convolutional network with a structured feature selection mechanism, we can learn to distill a subset of latent visual features that are simultaneously highly predictive for ranking and statistically indistinguishable across protected groups.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage convolutional architecture. First, a backbone CNN (e.g., ResNet) extracts a high-dimensional latent feature set. Second, a selective gating module, trained with a combined loss, performs feature selection. The loss integrates: (1) a listwise ranking loss (e.g., ListNet) for predictive performance; (2) a multi-group fairness loss inspired by the *HappyMap* multicalibration framework, ensuring calibration of ranking scores conditioned on both the selected feature subset and group membership. Optimization will use techniques for differentiable subset selection, ensuring the selected features are both fair and predictive.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified optimization framework for fair, interpretable visual ranking. It will yield a method that not only produces fair rankings but also outputs the specific visual features (e.g., techniques, tools) deemed fair and predictive, enhancing auditability. This bridges the gap between multicalibration theory and high-dimensional computer vision applications.
proposals.novelty: Unlike standard fair representation learning which transforms entire embeddings, our approach adds a selective, in-process feature distillation explicitly optimized for a ranking objective under multicalibration constraints, moving fairness from the output space to the feature selection mechanism itself.

proposals.title: Fair Convolutional Representations via Multi-Group Multicalibration
proposals.motivation: The application of algorithmic fairness and privacy constraints to high-dimensional, perceptual data like images remains a significant challenge. Pre-2026 work on multi-group fairness and multicalibration has been largely theoretical or applied to tabular data, leaving a gap for complex, non-linear models like convolutional neural networks (CNNs) that dominate computer vision. Adapting these rigorous fairness frameworks to such architectures is crucial for deploying equitable systems in sensitive domains like facial analysis, medical imaging, and autonomous driving.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the multi-group fairness and multicalibration principles, particularly as formalized in frameworks like HappyMap, can be effectively integrated into the training of deep convolutional networks to learn high-dimensional representations that provably satisfy fairness constraints across many overlapping subgroups, even when subgroups are defined by complex, image-derived attributes.
proposals.method: We will adapt the multicalibration optimization framework to operate on the latent representations learned by a CNN backbone (e.g., ResNet). The core technical innovation is to formulate the multicalibration auditor as a lightweight, trainable network module that operates on these representations, identifying and correcting miscalibrated predictions for a rich family of subgroups defined via functions in a specified class. The training will alternate between updating the main CNN for task performance and updating the auditor network to minimize a multicalibration loss, drawing inspiration from the interplay between pseudorandomness and multi-group fairness. We will explore differential privacy for the training process to protect individual data in the sensitive visual dataset.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first method to achieve provable multi-group fairness guarantees for deep convolutional networks on image data. It will extend the theoretical reach of multicalibration to high-dimensional, non-linear function classes. The resulting open-source framework will enable practitioners in computer vision to audit and enforce subgroup fairness with mathematical rigor.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious element is the successful integration of a theoretically-grounded, post-hoc calibration algorithm into the end-to-end gradient-based training of a CNN, treating the fairness constraint as a trainable, adversarial game on the learned feature manifold, rather than as a simple penalty on tabular model outputs.

proposals.title: Pioneer-Climax Fairness: A Resource-Aware Framework for Edge-Based Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of edge computing for sensitive applications like hiring or credit scoring demands algorithmic fairness guarantees. However, existing fairness-aware models are computationally intensive, making them infeasible for deployment on resource-constrained edge hardware. This creates a tension between the need for sophisticated fairness interventions and the practical limitations of the deployment environment.
proposals.hypothesis: A two-stage framework inspired by ecological succession, where a lightweight 'pioneer' model enforces initial fairness constraints locally, followed by a conditional, resource-aware invocation of a powerful 'climax' model, can provide rigorous multi-group fairness guarantees while respecting strict edge device constraints.
proposals.method: We will formalize the framework using concepts from multicalibration and differential privacy. The pioneer model will be an ultra-lightweight, differentially private algorithm that produces a compressed, fairness-annotated sketch (e.g., using HappyMap-style multicalibration for a small set of protected groups). The climax model will be a more complex multicalibrated predictor or a fair representation learner. A decision function, based on device state and the pioneer's confidence, will govern if and how the sketch is transmitted for climax processing, ensuring the system's fairness properties compose rigorously across stages.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal framework for composable, resource-adaptive fairness on the edge. It will yield new algorithmic designs that explicitly trade off computational complexity with fairness strength, and offer a novel perspective on fairness as a temporally and spatially distributed process.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the ecological analogy structuring the fairness computation, moving from a monolithic model to a staged, conditional process. This explicitly bridges the gap between rigorous algorithmic fairness theory and the systems-level constraints of edge deployment, a connection largely unexplored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Fair, Private, and Lightweight Expert Discovery via On-Device Multi-Source Multicalibration
proposals.motivation: Expert finding in edge computing environments is hampered by data sparsity, privacy concerns, and resource constraints. Centralized, data-hungry models are infeasible, and existing decentralized approaches lack rigorous fairness guarantees and efficient mechanisms for fusing heterogeneous, on-device evidence without compromising individual privacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a lightweight, on-device learning algorithm can synthesize multiple sparse local data sources into a fair and useful expert representation by adapting and extending the principles of multicalibration and differential privacy to the edge computing paradigm.
proposals.method: We will develop a federated, edge-native algorithm where each device holds multiple sparse data sources (e.g., local docs, usage logs). The core technical innovation is a lightweight variant of multicalibration (inspired by HappyMap) applied per-device to ensure fairness across protected groups defined by the local data context. Model updates will be shared via differentially private mechanisms, and the fusion of multi-source evidence will be framed as a multi-group fairness problem, ensuring the final expert model is calibrated across intersections defined by data source and group membership.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute the first framework for fairness-constrained, privacy-preserving expert discovery designed for resource-constrained edges. It will extend the theory of multicalibration to multi-source, decentralized settings and provide a practical algorithm demonstrating that rigorous individual fairness and privacy can be achieved without centralized data aggregation.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in applying multicalibration—a fairness method typically used in centralized settings—as a lightweight, on-device regularizer for fusing multiple private data sources, creating a unified framework that jointly addresses sparsity, fairness, and privacy at the edge.

proposals.title: Certified Bias Audits via Constrained Adversarial Inversion of Learned Representations
proposals.motivation: As algorithmic decision-making proliferates, rigorous methods to audit models for potential unfairness are critical. Current fairness audits often rely on existing data or unconstrained adversarial examples, which may fail to expose biases inherent in the model's learned mapping or produce unrealistic inputs. A method to systematically probe a fixed model for its worst-case, in-distribution fairness violations is needed for trustworthy deployment.
proposals.hypothesis: Formalizing bias extraction as a constrained inverse problem in the model's latent representation space can yield a certified upper bound on the model's potential fairness violation, providing a more reliable and interpretable audit than existing statistical or unconstrained adversarial methods.
proposals.method: We propose an optimization framework that searches the latent space of a fixed model (e.g., the penultimate layer of a neural network) for representations which, when passed through the final decision layer, maximize a specified multi-group fairness metric (e.g., calibration error, equalized odds difference). The search is constrained by a reconstruction loss, ensuring the corresponding synthetic input remains within a small epsilon-ball of a realistic data point in feature space. This builds upon and extends the multi-group fairness and multicalibration frameworks (e.g., HappyMap) by treating the model as a fixed mapping and using constrained optimization—potentially leveraging tools from differential privacy for stability—to perform a targeted inversion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new, rigorous tool for model developers to perform internal red-teaming, yielding a certificate of maximum bias. It advances the field of algorithmic fairness from post-hoc measurement on given datasets to proactive, certified evaluation of model vulnerability. The method also offers interpretability by revealing the synthetic, yet realistic, inputs that trigger the worst-case behavior.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior fairness audits, this approach explicitly constrains the adversarial search to the model's own learned representation manifold, ensuring discovered biases are attributable to the model's parameters, not out-of-distribution artifacts. It connects adversarial robustness techniques directly to certified multi-group fairness guarantees.

proposals.title: Adversarial Subgroup Synthesis: A Stress-Testing Framework for Multi-Group Fairness
proposals.motivation: Current fairness audits are limited to evaluating models on pre-defined, often coarse, protected attributes. This fails to reveal biases against intersectional or latent subgroups not explicitly recorded in the data, creating a false sense of security. A proactive, rigorous method is needed to discover a model's worst-case unfairness, analogous to stress-testing materials to find breaking points.
proposals.hypothesis: By treating a model's internal representations as a material to be stressed, an optimization framework can synthesize novel, high-stakes subgroup definitions that maximize unfairness metrics, revealing more severe vulnerabilities than evaluations on fixed attributes.
proposals.method: We will develop an optimization framework that solves the inverse problem of finding a subgroup membership function—defined via linear combinations or small decision trees on features or latent representations—to maximize a target disparity (e.g., equalized odds gap). The core will adapt and extend multicalibration and fairness auditing algorithms (e.g., from 'HappyMap') to an adversarial, generative setting. We will employ gradient-based search for differentiable definitions and bandit or boosting techniques for combinatorial feature spaces, ensuring subgroups are interpretable and non-trivial.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a novel diagnostic tool for model developers to proactively identify severe fairness failures. It will advance the theory of multi-group fairness by characterizing the space of subgroups where guarantees can and cannot hold. The framework will also yield a new benchmark for comparing the robustness of fair learning algorithms.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior work auditing fixed groups or using random perturbations, this actively engineers the worst-case subgroup via optimization. It bridges the adversarial robustness and fairness communities by applying a stress-testing paradigm, moving from verification of known groups to discovery of unknown vulnerabilities.

proposals.title: Adversarial Representation Extraction: A Framework for Auditing Model Fairness via Maximized Unfairness
proposals.motivation: Current fairness auditing methods often measure a model's deviation from a specific fairness criterion. This is reactive and may miss worst-case vulnerabilities. A proactive, adversarial approach is needed to stress-test models by constructing the most discriminatory representations possible, revealing latent biases and failure modes not captured by standard metrics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that formulating and solving the inverse problem—explicitly maximizing a formal measure of unfairness subject to model fidelity constraints—will yield a powerful diagnostic tool that can systematically uncover a model's most severe fairness violations and its sensitivity to adversarial subgroup constructions.
proposals.method: We will frame the problem as an optimization: given a trained model, find a representation (e.g., a derived attribute or a weighting of individuals) that maximizes a multi-group unfairness measure (e.g., from multicalibration or multi-accuracy). The method will build on Cynthia Dwork's recent work on multi-group fairness and HappyMap, treating the fairness auditor as an adversarial player. We will develop iterative algorithms, potentially inspired by multiplicative weights or gradient-based attacks, to solve this constrained optimization, ensuring the adversarial representation remains statistically plausible within the data distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, rigorous framework for model stress-testing, moving fairness auditing from verification to vulnerability discovery. It will yield concrete algorithms to generate 'worst-case' fairness reports and could inform the design of more robust, adversarially fair models. The extracted representations themselves will serve as interpretable artifacts highlighting problematic data correlations.
proposals.novelty: While prior work defines and minimizes unfairness, directly maximizing it as an inverse problem for auditing is novel. The approach synthesizes Dwork's multi-group fairness theory with an adversarial, diagnostic mindset, differing from the predominant goal of achieving fairness.

proposals.title: Algorithmic Niche Width: A Kernel Bandwidth Tuning Framework for the Fairness-Expressiveness Trade-off
proposals.motivation: The fairness-expressiveness trade-off is a fundamental tension in algorithmic fairness, where enforcing group fairness constraints can degrade a model's overall accuracy or fidelity. Pre-2026 work, including Dwork's on multi-group fairness and multicalibration, seeks to manage this trade-off but often in complex, high-dimensional models. There is a need for principled, interpretable mechanisms to navigate this Pareto frontier in simpler, widely-used non-parametric methods like kernel-based models and k-NN, where the tuning of model 'resolution' is currently ad-hoc with respect to fairness.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the bandwidth parameter in kernel and distance-based models can be formally treated as an 'algorithmic niche width,' analogous to ecological niche theory, and that explicitly optimizing it within a multi-objective framework provides a continuous, interpretable control for balancing group fairness and model expressiveness.
proposals.method: We will formulate a bi-objective optimization problem where one objective is a group fairness metric (e.g., demographic parity difference across protected groups) and the other is a measure of expressiveness/fidelity (e.g., empirical risk or a complexity measure). For an RBF kernel SVM or a k-NN classifier, the kernel/radius bandwidth will be the primary tuning variable. We will adapt multi-group fairness and multicalibration audit techniques from pre-2026 literature to evaluate fairness violations as a function of bandwidth. The Pareto-optimal frontier will be characterized using standard multi-objective optimization algorithms, and we will analyze the theoretical connection between bandwidth, smoothness, and fairness using concepts from algorithmic stability and learning theory.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, biologically-inspired conceptual framework ('algorithmic niche') for understanding model specialization. It will deliver a concrete, tunable method for practitioners to explicitly navigate fairness-accuracy trade-offs in interpretable, non-parametric models. The resulting analysis will contribute new insights into how model resolution inherently interacts with multi-group fairness guarantees.
proposals.novelty: While kernel bandwidth selection is well-studied for performance, its systematic optimization for fairness is novel. The ecological analogy provides a fresh, principled justification for the trade-off, moving beyond ad-hoc regularization. This approach directly connects multi-group fairness objectives to a fundamental model hyperparameter in a way not previously explored in Dwork's or others' pre-2026 work on multicalibration and fairness.

proposals.title: Local Fairness Audits for Citation Graphs via Multicalibration and Kernel Methods
proposals.motivation: Global citation metrics like PageRank are monolithic and can be gamed, potentially reinforcing biases and creating insular citation cartels that distort the perceived authority and impact of scientific work. This undermines the integrity of scholarly evaluation. A pre-2026 gap exists in auditing these dynamics with the nuanced, individual-level fairness frameworks developed for algorithmic decision systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The authority conferred by a citation neighborhood can be audited for local fairness properties, and systematic deviations from multicalibration—where a paper's influence is not representative of similar papers in the intellectual landscape—reveal manipulable or biased structures in the graph.
proposals.method: We will adapt the HappyMap multicalibration framework to operate on local neighborhoods within the citation graph. For a target paper or query, we will define a similarity kernel (e.g., based on textual embeddings or graph proximity) to construct a relevant subgraph. We then treat the citation-based authority score (e.g., a local PageRank variant) as a predictor to be audited. The method will test for calibration and multi-group fairness across protected attributes defined by metadata (venue, author institution, topic) within this local context, using the iterative boosting-style updates of HappyMap to quantify the audit's findings.
proposals.contribution: This will produce a novel, dynamic audit tool that diagnoses fairness violations in citation authority at a local, context-sensitive level, moving beyond global rankings. It formally bridges the literature on algorithmic fairness and bibliometrics, providing a mathematical framework to quantify and reason about distortion and insularity in scientific influence.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in treating citation graphs not as networks to be ranked, but as systems to be audited for individual-level fairness using local, non-parametric models, directly applying Dwork's recent work on multicalibration to a new domain.

proposals.title: Distributed Lagrangian Optimization with Differential Privacy and Multi-Group Fairness Guarantees
proposals.motivation: Modern machine learning increasingly relies on distributed optimization over massive datasets, often involving sensitive personal information. Pre-2026 work highlights the critical need to protect individual privacy and ensure fairness across multiple subgroups within such distributed computations. Existing distributed methods often treat optimization, privacy, and fairness as separate concerns, lacking a unified framework that scales efficiently while providing rigorous, composable guarantees.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a distributed Lagrangian optimization framework can be designed to efficiently solve large-scale constrained learning problems, where the constraints enforce multi-group fairness, while the distributed computation itself provably satisfies differential privacy for individual data points.
proposals.method: We will extend the Lagrangian method underlying multicalibration and fairness algorithms to a distributed setting. The core technical approach involves a master-worker architecture where local updates on data shards are performed using privatized gradient computations (via DP-SGD or the exponential mechanism) and coordinated to solve a global Lagrangian. We will integrate privacy accounting across rounds using composition theorems and ensure fairness constraints are satisfied at the global level by designing a distributed variant of the constraint-relaxation technique from multi-group fairness and HappyMap.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable, unified algorithm for private and fair learning on distributed data. It will contribute new composition theorems for differential privacy in distributed Lagrangian optimization and demonstrate how multi-group fairness constraints can be enforced in this architecture, bridging the gap between large-scale systems and individual-level ethical guarantees.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious integration lies in making the Lagrangian coordination mechanism—critical for enforcing multi-group fairness—itself privacy-preserving in a distributed context, avoiding the leakage of sensitive subgroup information during the global aggregation phase.

proposals.title: Private Data Synthesis Under Adversarial Priors: A Framework for Robust Distributional Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: The standard model for private data synthesis assumes a uniform or benign prior over possible microdata, which is often unrealistic. In practice, an adversary may have strong prior beliefs about individuals, making privacy guarantees based on uniform randomness vulnerable. This work addresses a core limitation in differential privacy when applied to data generation, ensuring robustness against an adversary with arbitrary, non-uniform prior knowledge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by formally modeling an adversary's prior distribution over the data domain, we can design synthesis algorithms that provide rigorous differential privacy guarantees even under worst-case, adversarial priors, and that this will reveal new connections between privacy, fairness under distribution shift, and multicalibration.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework of differential privacy to condition on a rich class of adversarial prior distributions. The technical approach will involve: 1) Defining a new privacy measure, Prior-Robust Differential Privacy, which integrates the adversary's prior into the privacy loss random variable. 2) Developing synthesis algorithms by adapting the exponential mechanism and the propose-test-release framework to be prior-aware, using techniques from Bayesian inference and robust statistics. 3) Leveraging multicalibration methods (e.g., HappyMap) to ensure the synthetic data maintains statistical validity across subgroups even when the prior is misspecified.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more realistic and robust foundation for private data release, moving beyond the uniform prior assumption. It will yield new algorithmic tools for data curators facing adversaries with strong side information and create a formal bridge between robust privacy definitions and multigroup fairness under distribution shift.
proposals.novelty: While prior work in differential privacy often assumes a worst-case neighboring dataset, it typically does not formalize worst-case prior beliefs *about* the data. This proposal explicitly challenges the uniform microdata distribution model, integrating adversarial priors into the core algorithmic design in a way that pre-2026 literature has not systematically explored.

proposals.title: Multicalibrated Synthesis: Validating Utility for Complex Policy-Relevant Analyses
proposals.motivation: The adoption of differentially private (DP) synthetic data for policy research is hindered by narrow utility evaluations, often limited to low-order statistics. Pre-2026, there is a critical need to understand if synthetic data preserves validity for complex, real-world analyses like causal inference, distributional comparisons, and multi-group fairness audits, which are essential for equitable policy design. Current methods lack a principled framework to measure and guarantee utility across this broader analytical spectrum.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that synthetic data generators incorporating multi-group fairness and multicalibration constraints—extending beyond marginal preservation—will produce data that maintains utility for a wider class of policy-relevant statistical analyses while providing rigorous differential privacy guarantees.
proposals.method: We will extend the HappyMap multicalibration framework to guide the synthesis process, ensuring calibrated predictions for a rich class of functions (e.g., regression estimators, disparity measures) across many overlapping subpopulations. We will develop a DP synthesis algorithm that iteratively corrects for miscalibration using a selection mechanism from the sparse vector technique. Utility will be rigorously tested on real datasets via a benchmark suite of analyses, including logistic regression, propensity score matching for treatment effects, and fairness metrics across protected groups, comparing against baseline DP synthesis methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel DP synthesis algorithm with multicalibration guarantees, 2) a comprehensive benchmark and methodology for evaluating synthetic data utility in policy contexts, and 3) theoretical and empirical evidence linking multicalibration to utility preservation for complex analyses. This bridges the gap between privacy and practical data science needs.
proposals.novelty: While multicalibration has been used for fair prediction, its application to guide the *generation* of DP synthetic data for complex analyses is novel. This moves beyond preserving marginals to preserving the validity of analytical relationships, a non-obvious extension critical for policy work.

proposals.title: Robust Multi-Signal Expertise Verification via Adversarial Multicalibration
proposals.motivation: Current expertise verification, especially in online and decentralized settings, relies heavily on document-based metrics (e.g., publications, citations) that are susceptible to manipulation, collusion, and bias. This creates unreliable systems for peer review, hiring, and content moderation. A principled, multi-faceted, and robust alternative is needed to synthesize diverse evidence streams while guaranteeing resilience against strategic gaming.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying a framework of adversarial multicalibration to a synthesis of multiple non-textual expertise signals, we can produce an expertise assessment that is both accurate and provably robust to a defined class of coordinated manipulation attempts.
proposals.method: We will formalize signals (peer endorsements, artifact creation traces, outcome metrics) as functions from individuals to a feature space. A HappyMap-style multicalibration algorithm will be used to synthesize them into a calibrated predictor. The core novelty is embedding an adversarial auditor: a red-team that continuously generates simulated attacks (e.g., Sybil endorsements, artifact forgery) against the current model. The multicalibration procedure will be iteratively constrained to satisfy calibration guarantees not just on the original data distribution, but also on these adversarially generated distributions, hardening the system.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal, auditable framework for robust multi-signal aggregation, extending fairness and calibration concepts to security and manipulation resistance. It will yield concrete algorithms for red-teaming automated decision systems and a new theoretical connection between differential privacy-style adversarial reasoning and multicalibration for group fairness.
proposals.novelty: While multicalibration ensures fairness across subgroups, its application to defend against *coordinated, strategic adversaries* aiming to game multiple signals is unexplored. This merges Dwork's lines of work on algorithmic fairness, individual guarantees, and adversarial analysis in privacy into a new domain of expertise verification.

proposals.title: The Efficient Frontier of Rankings: A Multi-Objective, Context-Aware Framework for Individualized Utility
proposals.motivation: Current ranking systems typically produce a single, context-free ordering, implicitly assuming a universal notion of 'best.' This fails to account for diverse user goals, which may prioritize different trade-offs between criteria like accuracy, novelty, or fairness. A formal framework for generating contextually optimal rankings is needed, particularly as algorithmic rankings influence critical domains from information access to hiring.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting Modern Portfolio Theory's concept of an efficient frontier, we can construct a Pareto-optimal surface of rankings representing optimal trade-offs between multiple objectives. A user's specific context, expressed via a utility function, can then be used to select the single, formally optimal ranking from this frontier.
proposals.method: We will define a multi-objective ranking optimization problem with criteria like relevance, diversity, and group fairness. Using multi-objective optimization techniques, we will algorithmically construct the 'ranking frontier.' To personalize, we will draw on the multicalibration framework (as in HappyMap) to learn or infer a user's utility function from stated preferences or implicit feedback, ensuring the mapping from context to the frontier is accurate and robust. The final ranking is the point on the frontier maximizing this personalized utility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, mathematical framework for context-aware ranking, moving beyond one-size-fits-all solutions. It will formally connect portfolio optimization to algorithmic fairness and personalization, offering new tools to audit and control the trade-offs inherent in ranked outputs.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the direct analogy to Modern Portfolio Theory's efficient frontier, applied to the structured space of rankings, combined with a multicalibration-based method for context elicitation to select the individually optimal point.

proposals.title: Adversarial Context Elicitation: A Multicalibration Framework for Auditing Personalized Rankings
proposals.motivation: Personalized ranking systems, from search to recommendations, make critical decisions that shape individual opportunity and perception. Current fairness audits often evaluate aggregate outcomes or predefined subgroups, potentially missing severe failures for specific, nuanced user contexts. A method to proactively identify the precise contexts where personalization logic breaks down is needed to move from post-hoc measurement to targeted hardening.
proposals.hypothesis: By formulating the audit as an inverse optimization problem—searching for user contexts that maximize a multicalibration-style fairness violation—we can algorithmically discover the most vulnerable points in a ranking model's logic, which are not apparent from standard subgroup analyses.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the multicalibration and HappyMap frameworks. For a fixed ranking model, we treat the user context as an input to be optimized. Using gradient-based or bandit optimization techniques, we will search the context space (e.g., combinations of stated intents, protected attributes, and historical interactions) to elicit contexts that maximize a disparity function, such as the difference in average rank utility between relevant protected groups. The core algorithm will iteratively refine a 'map' from contexts to fairness violations, producing a set of high-disparity adversarial contexts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel algorithmic audit tool for ranking systems, producing actionable 'stress tests' in the form of adversarial contexts. It will advance the theory of multicalibration by applying it to an inverse, adversarial search problem, connecting individual fairness concerns with the discovery of systematic multi-group failures.
proposals.novelty: While prior work audits for fairness given a context, this inverts the problem to find the worst-case contexts. It fuses the adversarial robustness perspective with the nuanced fairness guarantees of multicalibration, targeting the specific failure modes of personalized systems rather than static classifiers.

proposals.title: Fairness-Constrained Variational Autoencoders for Debiasing Static Citation Graphs
proposals.motivation: Citation graphs are a primary mechanism for establishing scientific authority, yet they are known to encode and amplify societal biases, disadvantaging authors from underrepresented groups. This distorts the perceived landscape of influence, perpetuates inequity, and undermines the integrity of scholarly evaluation. Existing work on fairness in rankings or graph algorithms often focuses on post-hoc correction or detection, rather than modeling the underlying generative process of citations to propose equitable alternatives.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating group fairness constraints directly into the latent space optimization of a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) trained on a static citation graph, we can learn a representation that disentangles genuine scholarly influence from bias, enabling the generation of a debiased network that satisfies specified fairness criteria while preserving core structural properties.
proposals.method: We will develop a VAE architecture where the encoder maps nodes (authors/papers) to a latent distribution. The reconstruction loss will capture the graph's adjacency structure. Crucially, we will augment the VAE's objective function with a fairness regularizer, such as a penalty derived from the multi-group fairness or multicalibration framework (e.g., HappyMap) applied to latent representations or reconstructed link probabilities across protected groups (e.g., author demographics). This forces the model to generate citations that are conditionally independent of group membership given merit. We will explore constraints for demographic parity and equality of opportunity in citation reception.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, generative framework for auditing and repairing bias in network structures, moving from identification to active mitigation. It will yield a method to produce synthetic 'fair' citation graphs for downstream analysis and policy simulation. The project will also advance the integration of rigorous algorithmic fairness definitions, like multi-group fairness, into deep generative models for structured data.
proposals.novelty: While VAEs have been used for graph generation and fairness has been studied in rankings, the fusion of a VAE's generative capacity with in-process, multi-group fairness constraints for debiasing an existing static network is a novel direction. This approach directly models the counterfactual question of what the citation graph would look like under a fair generative process.

proposals.title: Fairness-Constrained Disentanglement for Topic-Independent Expertise Representation
proposals.motivation: Current models of expertise are often domain-specific, conflating deep reasoning ability with familiarity with a particular topic's conventions and facts. This makes it difficult to assess and compare fundamental problem-solving skills across disciplines or to predict performance on novel, interdisciplinary tasks. A principled, mathematical framework for isolating and measuring transferable expertise could improve fairness in evaluation and enable better matching of individuals to complex, cross-domain challenges.
proposals.hypothesis: A variational autoencoder (VAE) can learn a disentangled latent representation of expert outputs where a 'core skill' vector, regularized for topic invariance via algorithmic fairness constraints, provides a predictive, portable measure of fundamental reasoning ability that generalizes across domains.
proposals.method: We will develop a VAE architecture with a latent space partitioned into a core skill vector z_core and domain-specific vectors z_domain. Inspired by β-VAE and group fairness notions, we will impose a multi-group fairness constraint (e.g., using a multicalibration-inspired regularizer like HappyMap) on z_core to enforce statistical independence from identifiable topic groups in the training data. The training objective will combine reconstruction loss, disentanglement penalties, and the fairness regularizer, ensuring z_core captures only topic-invariant features predictive of expertise.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel formalization of 'topic-independent expertise' as a fairness-constrained representation learning problem, 2) a concrete neural framework for disentangling and measuring portable skill, and 3) new connections between algorithmic fairness for groups and the learning of invariant individual representations.
proposals.novelty: While disentangled representation learning and fairness in ML are active areas, applying multi-group fairness constraints as a direct mechanism to enforce topic invariance for creating a portable, individual expertise fingerprint is a novel synthesis. It moves beyond typical fairness goals of preventing discrimination to actively construct a useful, equitable representation.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Multi-Group Fairness: Auditing and Mitigating Temporal Disparities in Expertise Networks
proposals.motivation: Current algorithmic fairness frameworks largely evaluate fairness at a single point in time, ignoring the dynamic processes that lead to observed disparities. In domains like scientific citation, expertise is not static; it evolves through feedback loops where recognition begets further recognition (the 'Matthew Effect'). A pre-2026 gap exists in formally defining and enforcing fairness constraints over these temporal trajectories, which is critical for diagnosing whether systemic inequities are embedded in the *process* of expertise development itself.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying multi-group fairness constraints, specifically multicalibration, to the temporal dynamics of expertise networks will reveal and enable the mitigation of longitudinal disparities—such as differential rates of recognition growth or decay—that are invisible to static fairness audits.
proposals.method: We will model expertise trajectories as time-series data (e.g., citation counts, publication records). We will adapt the HappyMap multicalibration framework to enforce fairness constraints not just on a final outcome, but on the *sequence* of predictions or recognitions over time. This involves defining temporally-aware loss functions that penalize disparities in the rate of change and stability of predicted influence across protected groups. The core algorithmic challenge is extending the calibration guarantees of HappyMap to ensure fairness across both groups and time steps, potentially using techniques from online learning and differential privacy to handle the sequential, adaptive nature of the data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal framework for longitudinal fairness in dynamic systems, moving the field from snapshot analysis to process auditing. It will yield novel metrics for temporal disparity and an adaptable algorithm for enforcing equity in evolving networks, with applications beyond academia to hiring, funding, and content recommendation systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing the rigorous, group-aware fairness of multicalibration with temporal sequence modeling, explicitly targeting the fairness of the *dynamics* (growth, decay) rather than just a static state, which is a significant departure from pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Fairness-Constrained Representation Learning for Multi-Attribute Expert Finding in Professional Corpora
proposals.motivation: Expert finding systems are increasingly deployed in high-stakes professional domains like law and medicine, where recommendations can influence case outcomes or patient care. Existing models, designed for academic citation networks, fail to address the complex, latent expertise signals and severe multi-dimensional biases (e.g., gender, seniority, geography in combination) present in corpora like legal databases or medical records. This creates a critical need for methods that ensure fair and robust expertise discovery in these sensitive, non-academic contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating multi-group fairness constraints directly into the joint representation learning process for documents and experts, we can develop expert finding systems for professional domains that are provably robust to intersectional biases while maintaining high accuracy.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend the multicalibration framework, specifically techniques from 'HappyMap', to the expert finding task. The core is a two-tower neural model that learns embeddings for documents and candidate experts from professional text.
